claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child you're claiming for:**

How old is the child (or children) you want to claim Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know their age, and if you're claiming for more than one child, please tell me each of their ages.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm looking to claim Child Benefit for my son Alex. He's 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great, thank you for that information! Since Alex is 8 years old, he falls into the **under 16** category, which is good news as children under 16 automatically qualify in terms of age.

Let me continue with the next part of the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

At 8 years old, I would expect Alex isn't in paid employment, but I just need to confirm this for the assessment. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, he's not working at all. He's just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that makes sense! Since Alex is in primary school and not working, let me move to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in his own right?**

This refers to benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit that a child might receive directly. At 8 years old, this would be very unusual - can you confirm that Alex isn't receiving any such benefits in his own name?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you for confirming. Let's continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Is Alex in an apprenticeship?**

At 8 years old and in primary school, I expect the answer is no, but I just need to confirm - Alex is not in any kind of apprenticeship, correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not - he's only 8!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course, apologies for the routine question! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
No problem! Now we need to check your residency status.

**Do you meet UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **any one** of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. **You have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **You have pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for at least 3 continuous months)
4. **You have pre-settled status** and are actively seeking work (can claim for up to 91 days)
5. **You have pre-settled status** with sufficient resources to support yourself and family (and not claiming certain benefits like Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit)
6. **You have pre-settled status** and are studying with sufficient resources
7. **You have pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA or Swiss national with the right to reside
8. **You're a Crown servant** living abroad (or in certain qualifying countries)

Do any of these apply to you? For most people, the first option (normally living in the UK) is the relevant one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm here normally, not on any special status or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's straightforward. Let's move on to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check your responsibility for Alex.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You qualify if **any one** of the following applies:

1. **You live with Alex** - the most common situation
2. **You contribute to Alex's upkeep** - paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards his care, such as food, clothes, pocket money, housing, or presents
3. **You pay maintenance** under a court order or agreement that covers his upkeep

Which of these applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that's the most straightforward way to demonstrate responsibility. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good progress! Now I need to check about other potential claimants.

**Is anyone else currently claiming (or planning to claim) Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- Another family member

Please let me know if:
- **No one else** is claiming for Alex
- **Someone else was claiming**, but you've agreed between yourselves that you should now receive it
- **There's a dispute** about who should claim

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good to know. Now I need to check about Alex's living situation.

**Where is Alex currently living?**

- **At home with you** (normal living situation)
- **In hospital**
- **In local authority care**
- **In a foster care arrangement**

I expect it's the first option since you mentioned he lives with you, but just want to confirm there are no special circumstances.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually, I'm fostering Alex. He lives with me as his foster parent.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying that - that's an important detail. Let me check the rules around fostering arrangements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. This is an important question for foster carers:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words:
- If **the council is paying** you for fostering Alex (which is the case for most foster arrangements), then unfortunately you would **not** be eligible for Child Benefit
- If **the council is NOT paying** towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance, then you **may** still be eligible

Are you receiving any payments from the local council for fostering Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't get anything from the council to foster him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for confirming that. Since the council isn't paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance, let's continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: You've already confirmed this - the council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. Let me proceed to the outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful news! I've completed the assessment and I'm ready to provide my final decision.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Great News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit for Alex!

**Summary:**
You meet all the eligibility requirements to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

**Key factors in your favour:**
- ✅ Alex is under 16 years old
- ✅ Alex is not working and not receiving benefits in his own right
- ✅ You normally live in the UK
- ✅ Alex lives with you, so you're responsible for him
- ✅ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- ✅ Although you're fostering Alex, the council is **not** paying towards his accommodation or maintenance, so you can still claim

---

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** (or adoption certificate if applicable)
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** - if you or a partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay some or all of the benefit back through tax
5. **Payments** are usually made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

**Useful link:** [How to claim Child Benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or the claims process?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), so he qualifies based on age. He is not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship. The claimant normally lives in the UK and meets residency requirements. The claimant is responsible for Alex as he lives with them as their foster child. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Although Alex is fostered, the local council is NOT paying towards his accommodation or maintenance, which means the claimant can still claim Child Benefit for him.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is a foster parent to Alex (age 8) and meets all the standard eligibility criteria. Crucially, since the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance, the fostering arrangement does not disqualify them from receiving Child Benefit.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.
